questions.text: I detect when someone's agreement comes with unspoken conditions or reservations.
I believe that emotional connection to others' suffering is what drives people to make the world better.
I would rather lead a risky venture than follow in a safe one.
I find that I am not naturally attuned to the way gossip functions as social bonding.
I handle administrative tasks in bursts when they become unavoidable.
I gently offer perspective but leave it to others to decide if they are off track.
I notice that my sleep quality is rarely affected by emotional events from the preceding day.
I find that setting firm boundaries around my personal time prevents burnout and keeps me effective.
I would rather make a controversial call than let the team drift without direction.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is what gives urgency to commitments that would otherwise remain abstract.
I feel that the future takes care of itself if you fully engage with the present.
I prefer to speak off the cuff because rehearsed delivery feels stiff to me.
I believe that decisions made from a position of emotional calm are more likely to be just than decisions made in the heat of emotional engagement.
I believe that building my own capabilities is the foundation of a good life.
I find deep satisfaction in making personal sacrifices for the benefit of my community.
I believe that my calm is structural, not performative — it goes all the way down.
I find that my emotional temperature swings widely within a single conversation.
When I feel content, the contentment saturates me completely — it's not a background hum, it's the whole song.
I notice that my emotions leave a physical imprint — tension headaches, tight shoulders, clenched fists.
I feel that my energy is best spent helping others reach their goals.
I hold the view that self-investment is not selfish—it's essential.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the conviction that has most consistently shaped the way I evaluate my own actions and their consequences.
I pick up on the way people's footsteps change when they are approaching someone they are nervous around.
I tend to miss when someone includes me in a plan out of obligation rather than desire.
I believe that approaching difficult human situations with deliberate objectivity is a mark of maturity, not of coldness.
I tend to miss the warning signs that a conversation is heading toward conflict.
I believe I have a talent for bringing people together around a shared purpose.
I experience the quiet moments of life — a still morning, a warm drink — with a gentle appreciation that never tips into intensity.
I hold the view that I should be the first to arrive and the last to leave when there is work to be done for others.
I sense when someone is using a story about another person to indirectly talk about themselves.
I tackle administrative tasks on a regular schedule so they never pile up.
I prefer to give clear directives rather than open-ended suggestions.
I typically let someone else introduce structure and then adapt to it.
I update my to-do list throughout the day as new tasks surface.
I believe that maintaining a dispassionate analytical stance during crises is a practice that over time has made me more effective and more trustworthy as a helper.
I build transition rituals between activities to stay grounded and focused.
